slides.title: Tie It Together and Prove It
slides.subtitle: Physical Vulnerabilities: a cumulative review across all three objectives — name the attacks, explain the harms, grade the risk — then the Lesson 2.2 quiz
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Lesson 2.2, Day 5 — the last day. No new essential knowledge today; instead we consolidate everything across all three objectives. 2.2.A, identify common physical attacks. 2.2.B, explain how threats exploit physical vulnerabilities. 2.2.C, assess and document risk. We'll run a worked review table that puts all three together, work a fresh cumulative scenario, do a vocab recap across the whole topic, and then you take the Lesson 2.2 quiz. The quiz spans the topic — five questions that can ask you to name an attack, explain a harm, or grade a risk. By the end of today you'll have proven you can do all three, which is exactly what the unit scenario at Xtensr asks of a real physical security analyst.

slides.prompt: One short story: A person in a contractor's vest carries a ladder up to a propped-open side door of an office, nods to a passing employee, walks in, photographs a sticky note with a Wi-Fi password on a monitor, then leaves through the same door past an unsecured electrical panel.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: name every physical attack or harm you can spot in this story, and for the single worst exposure, state whether you'd rate it high, moderate, or low risk.
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own — and this one pulls the whole topic together. A person in a contractor's vest carries a ladder to a propped side door, nods to an employee, walks in, photographs a sticky note with a Wi-Fi password on a monitor, and leaves past an unsecured electrical panel. In three to five sentences, name every physical attack or harm you can spot — there are several — and for the single worst exposure, tell me high, moderate, or low. Hold your answer. By the end of class you'll be able to dissect a story like this cleanly, which is precisely what the quiz asks.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 5 Guided Notes are a cumulative review across all three objectives, then quiz prep.

sections.title: The Whole Topic in One Table
sections.description: Attacks (2.2.A), harms (2.2.B), and the risk method (2.2.C) consolidated side by side for fast recall (EK 2.2.A.1–C.4).

sections.title: Cumulative Scenario + Vocab Recap
sections.description: Work one mixed scenario end to end and recap the topic's key terms before the quiz (EK 2.2.A.1–C.4).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.2 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Two sections today, both review. First, the whole topic in one table: the named attacks, the harms, and the risk method consolidated side by side so you can recall them fast. Second, a cumulative scenario worked end to end plus a vocab recap across the entire topic, right before the quiz. Keep the packet open — today's notes are your study sheet for the quiz and the unit exam.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how threats can exploit common physical vulnerabilities to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction to assets (LO 2.2.B)
Assess and document risks from physical vulnerabilities (LO 2.2.C)

slides.script: Today reviews all three objectives at once. LO 2.2.A: identify common physical attacks — the five named attacks and the social engineering behind them. LO 2.2.B: explain how threats, human and natural, exploit physical vulnerabilities to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction. LO 2.2.C: assess and document risks — grading high, moderate, low. The quiz at the end of class can pull from any of the three, sometimes in a single question, so today is about moving fluidly among them. By the end you'll be able to take a mixed scenario, name the attack, explain the harm, and grade the risk in one pass.

slides.label: The Whole Topic in One Table
slides.script: Section nine of the lesson — cumulative review across all three objectives. We start by putting the entire topic in front of you at once, then drill the connections. Twelve minutes.

slides.heading: Topic 2.2 on One Page: Attack → Harm → Risk
slides.subheading: The whole topic consolidated — recognize the attack, explain the harm, grade the risk.
rows: Manipulating a person, not a lock, to gain access/info
A human is tricked or exploited
Authorized person KNOWINGLY tricked into granting access
Heavy box, fake badge story, fake maintenance
Following close behind WITHOUT the person's awareness
Watching/recording a user access sensitive info
Eyes or a camera on a live screen/keypad
Going through physical trash for useful info
Copying an authorized user's access card
Don't forget floods, fires, storms count
Power cut, data copied, malware port, device destroyed
Grade HIGH / MODERATE / LOW by value × likelihood
slides.footer: AP TIP: Most exam items live in one row of this table. Harder items combine rows — e.g., name the attack (2.2.A), say what it enables (2.2.B), then grade the risk (2.2.C). Practice walking left to right across the rows.
slides.script: Here is the entire topic on one page — your study sheet. Top block, the attacks from 2.2.A: social engineering is the thread, EK 2.2.A.1; then piggybacking, the knowingly-tricked door, A.2; tailgating, followed unnoticed, A.3; shoulder surfing, eyes or camera on a live access, A.4; dumpster diving, the trash, A.5; card cloning, the copied badge, A.6. Each has a one-line exam tell in the last column — memorize those tells. Middle, 2.2.B: threats include natural disasters, B.1, don't forget that; and the harms — access, disruption, theft, destruction, modification — across B.2 through B.5. Bottom, 2.2.C: the risk method, grade by value times likelihood — sensitive and open is high, a foothold is moderate, low value and unlikely is low. Most exam questions live in a single row. The hard ones combine rows: name the attack, say what it enables, grade the risk. Practice reading left to right across a row — that's the full reasoning chain.

slides.heading: Three Objectives, Three Must-Know Moves
slides.subheading: One consolidated takeaway per learning objective — the spine of the whole topic.

items.title: Name the Attack (2.2.A)
items.summary: Recognize and name the five common physical attacks — piggybacking, tailgating, shoulder surfing, dumpster diving, card cloning — and the social engineering that sets most of them up.

items.title: Explain the Harm (2.2.B)
items.summary: Explain how threats — human or natural — exploit a vulnerability to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction: power cut off, data stolen or copied, malware via ports, devices destroyed.

items.title: Grade the Risk (2.2.C)
items.summary: Assess and document each vulnerability as high, moderate, or low using two factors — the sensitivity or value of the exposed asset and the likelihood it is exploited.

slides.script: Three objectives, three moves to own walking into the quiz. One, name the attack, 2.2.A: recognize and name the five physical attacks and the social engineering behind them. Two, explain the harm, 2.2.B: say how a threat — human or natural — exploits a vulnerability to cause loss, damage, disruption, or destruction. Three, grade the risk, 2.2.C: assess and document high, moderate, or low using the two factors, asset value and likelihood. Name, explain, grade. If you can do those three moves on any scenario, you can answer anything this topic throws at you. Write the three moves at the top of your study sheet.

slides.label: Cumulative Scenario + Vocab Recap
slides.script: Section ten. We work one mixed scenario end to end — name, explain, grade — then recap the vocabulary across the whole topic before the quiz. Fourteen minutes.

slides.heading: Cumulative Scenario: One Story, All Three Objectives
slides.subheading: Dissect the bell-ringer story the way the quiz will ask you to — attack, harm, risk.

left.title: Name & Explain (2.2.A, 2.2.B)
examples: Walks in past a propped door, unnoticed — tailgating (EK 2.2.A.3)
Contractor vest is the social-engineering prop (EK 2.2.A.1, A.2)
Photographs a password on a monitor — shoulder surfing (EK 2.2.A.4)
Passes an unsecured electrical panel — power disruption exposure (EK 2.2.B.3)

right.title: Grade & Document (2.2.C)
examples: Exposed Wi-Fi password photographed → HIGH: sensitive credential, openly visible (EK 2.2.C.2)
Unsecured electrical panel → HIGH: critical system, disrupts all services (EK 2.2.C.2)
Propped side door → MODERATE: a foothold into controlled space (EK 2.2.C.3)
Document each in the assessment with its level and justification (EK 2.2.C.1)

slides.footer: KEY SKILL: One story can contain several attacks AND several risk levels. Work it methodically — list every attack/harm (2.2.A, 2.2.B), then grade each exposure (2.2.C). Don't stop at the first thing you spot.
slides.script: Let's dissect the bell-ringer story the way the quiz will. Left side, name and explain. The person walks in past a propped door without being stopped — that's tailgating, EK 2.2.A.3 — and the contractor vest is the social-engineering prop, A.1 and A.2. Photographing a password off the monitor is shoulder surfing, A.4. And passing the unsecured electrical panel exposes power disruption, EK 2.2.B.3. Right side, grade and document. The photographed Wi-Fi password is a sensitive credential left in the open — high, EK 2.2.C.2. The unsecured electrical panel is a critical system that could take all services down — high, also C.2. The propped side door is a foothold into controlled space — moderate, C.3. And you document each with its level and justification, C.1. The key skill: one story holds several attacks and several risk levels. Don't stop at the first thing — list every attack and harm, then grade each. That's the full move the exam rewards.

slides.heading: Topic Vocabulary Recap (LO 2.2.A, 2.2.B, 2.2.C)

terms.definition: Piggybacking tricks an authorized person into KNOWINGLY granting access; tailgating follows WITHOUT their awareness — the test is whether they knowingly let the adversary in (EK 2.2.A.2, EK 2.2.A.3).

Shoulder surfing watches or records a live access to sensitive info; dumpster diving harvests it from physical trash — both steal information without entering a restricted area (EK 2.2.A.4, EK 2.2.A.5).
Copying an authorized user's access card to gain all the access that user has, while the original card keeps working (EK 2.2.A.6).
Anything that could cause harm or disruption — human adversaries AND natural disasters that damage, destroy, or disrupt computers, data, and services (EK 2.2.B.1).
Why physical access is dangerous: it can defeat firewalls, passwords, and other technical controls that only guard against network attacks (EK 2.2.C.1).

The documented grade for a vulnerability — high when sensitive systems are exposed without controlled access, moderate when a noncritical asset is a foothold, low when a low-value asset is unlikely to be exploited (EK 2.2.C.2).

slides.script: A fast vocab recap across the whole topic — these are the distinctions the exam loves. Piggybacking versus tailgating: knowingly granted versus followed unnoticed, EK 2.2.A.2 and A.3. Shoulder surfing versus dumpster diving: watching a live access versus harvesting the trash, A.4 and A.5. Card cloning: copied badge, original still works, A.6. Threat: remember it includes natural disasters, EK 2.2.B.1. Bypassing technical controls: why physical access is special, EK 2.2.C.1. And the high-moderate-low grade, EK 2.2.C.2. If you can rattle off each of these with its EK number, you're ready. All six on your study sheet.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Quiz Warm-Up
slides.subheading: Mixed practice across LO 2.2.A, 2.2.B, and 2.2.C. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: Write your own one-paragraph physical-security scenario that contains at least one named attack (2.2.A) and one specific harm (2.2.B). Then grade the worst exposure high, moderate, or low and justify it (2.2.C), citing an EK for each part.
In one sentence each, give the exam tell that separates: (a) piggybacking from tailgating, (b) shoulder surfing from dumpster diving, and (c) a high-risk finding from a moderate-risk finding.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. This is your final warm-up before the quiz.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent — your final warm-up. Question one: write your own short physical-security scenario with at least one named attack and one specific harm, then grade the worst exposure high, moderate, or low and justify it — cite an EK for each part. Writing your own scenario forces you to use all three objectives at once. Question two: give me the one-line tell that separates piggybacking from tailgating, shoulder surfing from dumpster diving, and a high finding from a moderate one. Those three distinctions are the most-tested in the topic. Then we take the quiz.

slides.script: Everything is on the site for final review. Run the Lesson 2.2 self-check one more time, then Exercise 9, a mixed cumulative scenario set that pulls from all three objectives, and Exercise 10, a vocabulary and EK drill for the whole topic. The Lesson 2.2 quiz is the five-question check you take in class today. If you want any of the week again at your own pace, the lesson video walks all of it slide for slide.

slides.heading: End of Lesson 2.2
todayPoints: Consolidated the whole topic: name the attack (2.2.A), explain the harm (2.2.B), grade the risk (2.2.C)
Worked a cumulative scenario end to end, finding multiple attacks and multiple risk levels in one story
Recapped the most-tested distinctions: piggybacking vs. tailgating, shoulder surfing vs. dumpster diving, high vs. moderate
nextPoints: Topic 2.3 — Protecting Physical Spaces: recommend controls to mitigate the risks you identified
You'll return to the Xtensr labs to fix the vulnerabilities you graded this week
Carry forward the high/moderate/low method — it decides what gets fixed first
slides.teaser: You found and graded the Xtensr labs' weaknesses. Next topic, you fix them: 2.3 is where you recommend the physical security controls that mitigate the very risks you documented this week.
slides.script: Lesson 2.2 is complete. You can do all three moves now: name the five physical attacks and the social engineering behind them, explain how threats — human and natural — exploit vulnerabilities to cause loss, damage, disruption, and destruction, and assess and document risk as high, moderate, or low. You proved it on the quiz and on the Xtensr assessment. Next is Topic 2.3, Protecting Physical Spaces, where you return to the Xtensr labs and recommend the controls that mitigate the exact risks you graded this week — your high-risk findings get fixed first. That high-moderate-low method carries straight forward. Great work this week; keep your study sheet, you'll want it for the unit exam.
